metadata.working_title: A Fast Approach for Structural and Evolutionary Analysis Based on Energetic Profile Protein Comparison

abstract_sketch.problem: Predicting protein similarity, function, and evolutionary relationships efficiently is a core challenge in structural bioinformatics, with existing tools relying on expensive structural alignment or atomic-distance computation.
abstract_sketch.gap: Structure-based methods (RMSD, TM-Score, GR-Align) are computationally demanding; deep-learning embeddings (TM-Vec) offer speed but lack interpretability and require structural training data that may underrepresent novel folds.
abstract_sketch.approach: We introduce energy profiles derived from a distance-dependent knowledge-based potential. Each protein is represented by a 210-dimensional vector capturing the summation of pairwise amino-acid interaction energies. A Compositional Profile of Energy (CPE) is estimated from sequence alone, while a Structural Profile of Energy (SPE) is computed from 3D coordinates. Manhattan distance between profiles quantifies protein dissimilarity, eliminating the need for alignment.
abstract_sketch.key_results: CPE achieves 100% accuracy on the CATH 251-domain benchmark in 1 second versus 67 seconds for TM-Vec, and discriminates SARS-CoV, SARS-CoV-2, and MERS-CoV spike glycoproteins with ARI = 0.95 at tree cut = 4 (CPE) and ARI = 1 at tree cut = 3 (SPE). Across 4,405 coronavirus protein models spanning 28 families, CPE and SPE approach 100% 1-NN classification accuracy. Energy-profile separation measures correlate significantly with network-based drug-combination scores for 65 antihypertensive drugs.
abstract_sketch.impact: The energy-profile framework provides fast, alignment-free, and physically interpretable protein comparison applicable to hierarchical classification, phylogeny beyond the twilight zone, large-scale proteome screening, and drug-combination prediction.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Protein similarity as gateway to function and evolution; repository growth vs. annotation gap.
Existing paradigms: sequence alignment (PSI-BLAST) and structural alignment (RMSD, TM-Score, GR-Align) vs. modern ML embeddings (TM-Vec).

Knowledge-based statistical potentials encode native-state energy; assumption that similar structures share similar total energy motivates our stronger hypothesis: similar proteins possess analogous energy profiles.
210-dimensional pairwise amino-acid energy vector as a protein descriptor; structural (SPE) and sequence-composition (CPE) variants.
Applications spanning SCOP hierarchy, twilight-zone phylogeny (ferritin-like superfamily), coronavirus spike classification, bacteriocins, and drug combinations.
Contributions: alignment-free protein comparison, sequence-derived energy proxy, superior accuracy/time trade-off, scalability to thousands of proteins.

sections.title: Related Work
content_bullets: Knowledge-based potentials: Sippl and successors; distance-dependent, dihedral, and accessible-surface-area potentials.
Energy landscape theory: Eisenberg energy-profile for sequence-structure compatibility; Wolynes spin-glass Hamiltonians.
Protein similarity metrics: RMSD, TM-Score, GR-Align graphlet degrees, Yau-Hausdorff distance.
Deep-learning embeddings: TM-Vec trained on structure pairs with TM-score supervision.
Structure prediction as enabler: AlphaFold2, OmegaFold, ESMFold.
Evolutionary networks: NeighborNet, phangorn, SplitTree; manual ferritin-like annotations by Malik and Puente-Lelievre.
Network-based drug-combination prediction: Cheng et al. separation measure on the human interactome.

subsections.title: Training dataset and filtering
content_bullets: PISCES non-redundant chains with pairwise identity < 50%, resolution < 1.6 Angstrom, R-factor < 0.25, length 40-1000 residues.
Removal of proteins overlapping with downstream test sets.

subsections.title: Distance-dependent knowledge-based potential
content_bullets: Heavy-atom representation; Delaunay tessellation via Qhull for contact identification.
30 distance shells of width 0.5 Angstrom starting at 0.75 Angstrom; 167 atom types.
Boltzmann-like energy E(d) with RT = 0.582 kcal/mole and weight sigma = 0.02.
Residue-residue energy Delta E(A,B) as sum over contacting atom pairs.

subsections.title: Structural Profile of Energy (SPE)
content_bullets: 210-dimensional vector indexed by unordered amino acid pairs.
Each entry is the summed Delta E over residue pairs of the corresponding amino-acid types.

subsections.title: Compositional Profile of Energy (CPE) from sequence
content_bullets: Predictor matrix P mapping amino-acid composition to pairwise energy content.
Row-wise optimization by least-squares over training set; solved with MATLAB Symbolic Math Toolbox.
CPE E_ij estimated from composition frequencies alone.

subsections.title: Dissimilarity measure
content_bullets: Manhattan distance between 210-dimensional profiles.
Drug-pair separation measure E_AB combining within-drug and cross-drug Manhattan distances.

subsections.title: Benchmark datasets and evaluation
content_bullets: ASTRAL40/ASTRAL95 from SCOPe v2.08 for hierarchical analysis.
CATH v4.2.0 subset of 251 domains from families 1.10.8.10 and 3.10.28.10 (lengths 44-854, mean 211).
Five SCOP superfamilies: a.4.5, b.55.1, d.17.4, d.15.1, b.1.1.
Ferritin-like superfamily a.25.1 with families a.25.1.1 and a.25.1.2.
CoV3D-derived 143 spike glycoprotein chains (80 SARS-CoV-2, 31 SARS-CoV, 32 MERS-CoV).
BAGEL 690 bacteriocins with length > 30 aa; Freiberger 4,405 coronavirus models across 28 families.
Metrics: 1-NN accuracy, F1, Adjusted Rand Index, Spearman correlation, bootstrap with 100 replicates.

subsections.title: Sequence-derived energy matches structural energy
content_bullets: Context: Structural energy requires a 3D model; we test whether sequence alone suffices.
Approach: Pearson correlation between total SPE and CPE energies on ASTRAL40 and ASTRAL95.
Finding: Strong correlation in both datasets; p < 10e-16. 96% of the 210 interaction types show |r| < 0.5 between residual and protein length.
Interpretation: Sequence-based energy is a reliable proxy robust to protein length.

subsections.title: Energy profiles encode SCOP hierarchy
content_bullets: Context: Does a single 210-dim vector capture class/fold/superfamily/family structure?
Approach: UMAP projections on ASTRAL40/95 folds a.100, a.104; superfamilies a.29.2, a.29.3; families a.25.1.0, a.25.1.2 with n_neighbors = 30 and min_dist = 0.1.
Finding: Domains within the same fold/superfamily/family cluster together for both CPE and SPE; intraclass distances are significantly lower than interclass distances.
Interpretation: Energy profiles compress hierarchical structural information without requiring alignment.

subsections.title: 1-NN classification on CATH and five SCOP superfamilies
content_bullets: Context: Benchmark against RMSD, TM-Score, GR-Align, Yau-Hausdorff, TM-Vec on the 251-domain CATH task and five SCOP superfamilies.
Approach: 1-NN classifier with each distance metric; hardware 2.4 GHz / 8 GB RAM.

Finding: CPE and TM-Vec reach 100% accuracy on CATH; CPE completes in 1 second versus 67 seconds for TM-Vec. Baselines range from 59.2% to 81.5%. On five SCOP superfamilies (Table 1), CPE, SPE, and TM-Vec all approach 100%.
Interpretation: Energy profiles match the best deep-learning embedding in accuracy while offering a 67-fold speed-up.

subsections.title: Alpha- and beta-globin discrimination
content_bullets: Context: Close evolutionary relatives with divergent functions test fine-grained resolution.
Approach: UMAP (n_neighbors = 13, min_dist = 0.1) of CPE, SPE, TM-Vec over 21 mammalian hemoglobins.
Finding: All three methods separate alpha and beta globins cleanly (Fig. 4C).
Interpretation: Pairwise energetic signatures recover subfamily-level functional divergence consistent with known energetic frustration patterns.

subsections.title: Phylogeny of the ferritin-like superfamily beyond the twilight zone
content_bullets: Context: Superfamily a.25.1 with two families a.25.1.1 and a.25.1.2; sequence similarity often below 20-35%.
Approach: NeighborNet networks and neighbor-joining trees from CPE, SPE, and TM-Vec; Spearman correlation against manually curated order (Malik; Puente-Lelievre).

Finding: All three methods cleanly split the two SCOP families; SPE achieves perfect Spearman correlation on a.25.1.1; TM-Vec shows a negative correlation on a.25.1.2 (Table 2); CPE and SPE outperform TM-Vec overall.
Interpretation: Energy profiles preserve evolutionary signal in regions where sequence alignment fails.

subsections.title: Clustering of SARS-CoV, SARS-CoV-2, and MERS-CoV spike glycoproteins
content_bullets: Context: 143 CoV3D spike chains (80 SARS-CoV-2, 31 SARS-CoV, 32 MERS-CoV) with closed RBD.
Approach: Compare MSA (ClustalW), RMSD, TM-Score, TM-Vec, CPE, SPE on UPGMA dendrograms; evaluate with ARI and 100-replicate bootstrap.

Finding: CPE reaches ARI = 0.95 at tree cut 4; TM-Vec reaches 0.87 at cut 5; MSA 0.49 at cut 3; SPE reaches ARI = 1 at cut 3; RMSD 0.73 at cut 6; TM-Score 0.56 at cut 4. Running times: CPE 0.9 s, SPE 3 min, TM-Vec 89 s, MSA 72 s, RMSD 70 min, TM-Score 9.7 h.
Interpretation: Energy profiles recover the SARS-CoV/SARS-CoV-2 versus MERS-CoV dichotomy with both higher accuracy and orders-of-magnitude lower runtime.

subsections.title: Bacteriocin classification and scalability
content_bullets: Context: 690 BAGEL bacteriocins spanning RiPPs, class II, and class III with length > 30 aa.

Approach: UMAP of CPE; compare CPE distances to TM-scores of AlphaFold2, OmegaFold, ESMFold, and TM-Vec; statistical testing via two-tailed t-test with 125,869 cross-class pairs, 111,349 same-class pairs, and 13,431 subclass1 pairs.
Finding: CPE partitions bacteriocins by BAGEL annotation; all three t-test comparisons give p < 10e-16 (Fig. 7B).
Interpretation: Predicted-structure TM-scores blur class boundaries while CPE cleanly separates RiPPs from class II/III.

subsections.title: Papain-like protease subgenus clustering
content_bullets: Context: PLPro domains across four Betacoronavirus subgenera: Sarbecovirus (n = 31), Nobecovirus (n = 11), Merbecovirus (n = 35), Embecovirus (n = 45).
Approach: UMAP of CPE, SPE, TM-Vec with n_neighbors = 13, min_dist = 0.5.
Finding: Each subgenus forms a distinct cluster in all three embeddings (Fig. 7C).
Interpretation: The 210-dimensional signature resolves genus-level structure within a single protein family.

subsections.title: Energy-based drug-combination scoring
content_bullets: Context: Cheng et al. defined a network proximity S_AB on the human protein-protein interactome; we define an analogous energy separation E_AB.
Approach: Compute E_AB over 65 antihypertensive drugs with complementary exposure and correlate with S_AB.
Finding: Strong Pearson correlation between E_AB and S_AB (Fig. 7D).
Interpretation: Sequence-only energy profiles recover the signal captured by interactome topology at a fraction of the computational cost.

subsections.title: Large-scale validation on 4,405 coronavirus models
content_bullets: Context: 28 families spanning 4,405 AlphaFold2 models from Freiberger et al.
Approach: 1-NN classification with CPE, SPE, and TM-Vec; processing-time-per-amino-acid scalability on ASTRAL95 subsets of 1,000-30,000 proteins.
Finding: Both CPE and SPE approach 100% accuracy and F1 (Table 3); CPE shows a gentler time-per-residue slope than TM-Vec across all subset sizes (Fig. 7E).
Interpretation: The method scales near-linearly and is suited to proteome-scale screening.

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Energy profiles recover hierarchical, evolutionary, and pharmacological structure without structural alignment.
CPE offers 100% accuracy with 1-second runtime on the CATH benchmark, outperforming the 59.2-81.5% range of classical alignment baselines.
Limitations: dependence on known structures for training the potential; possible sensitivity to folding heterogeneity beyond length; drug-combination application needs experimental validation.
Interpretability: pairwise amino-acid dimensions admit biophysical reading in contrast to TM-Vec embeddings.
Outlook: reweighting profiles for remote homology or drug-target affinity; integration with AlphaFold-scale screening.

sections.title: Conclusion
content_bullets: Energy profiles encode structural, functional, and evolutionary information with high fidelity.
Sequence-derived CPE mirrors structural SPE, enabling alignment-free proteome-scale analysis.
Future work: application to drug-target interaction prediction and de novo protein design.
